note.soap:
S: A 35-year-old adult female presents with a one-week history of burning urination without associated fever, flank pain, or vaginal discharge. She reports no allergies and denies pregnancy. The symptoms are consistent with lower urinary tract irritation or infection. Patient is not pregnant.

O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. No vital signs or physical findings can be assessed. No reported fever or systemic symptoms.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection (cystitis)  
  - Differential: Urethritis, vaginal irritation or infection, interstitial cystitis  
  - Red flags considered: No fever, no flank pain, no vaginal discharge, no pregnancy, which lowers suspicion for pyelonephritis or complicated UTI.

P:  
  - Disposition: Manage as outpatient with urgent care or primary care follow-up if symptoms worsen or fail to improve.  
  - Plan: Empiric antibiotic therapy with nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days, assuming no contraindications. If nitrofurantoin is contraindicated, consider trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or fosfomycin based on local resistance patterns and allergies. Recommend increased fluid intake and symptomatic relief with phenazopyridine if needed.  
  - Safety net: Advise patient to seek urgent care or emergency evaluation if she develops fever, flank pain, worsening symptoms, vaginal discharge, or if symptoms persist beyond 3 days after starting antibiotics.

Evidence (snippets used): None available; clinical decision based on structured answers and standard guidelines.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: IDSA Guideline: Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis
